name: Ipamorelin
summary: Ipamorelin is a selective pentapeptide growth hormone secretagogue (a GHRP) that mimics ghrelin at the GHS-R1a receptor to stimulate a pulse of growth hormone from the pituitary. It is notable for being the first GHRP-receptor agonist with selectivity for GH release comparable to GHRH, releasing GH with minimal effect on cortisol, ACTH, or prolactin.
mechanism:
Ipamorelin (sequence Aib-His-D-2-Nal-D-Phe-Lys-NH2) is a synthetic pentapeptide that acts as a selective agonist at the growth hormone secretagogue receptor type 1a (GHS-R1a), the same receptor activated by the endogenous hormone ghrelin. This receptor is highly expressed on somatotroph cells of the anterior pituitary. Receptor binding signals through Gq/11 proteins, activating phospholipase C, generating IP3 and DAG, and mobilizing intracellular calcium, which triggers exocytosis of GH-containing secretory granules into the circulation.

Unlike a GHRH analog, which acts on the GHRH receptor, ipamorelin works through the ghrelin/GHRP pathway. Pharmacological profiling with GHRP and GHRH antagonists showed that ipamorelin, like GHRP-6, stimulates GH via a GHRP-like receptor. Its defining feature is selectivity: in preclinical and human studies it produced a clean, GHRH-like pulse of GH without significantly raising cortisol, ACTH, or prolactin, even at doses far above those needed for maximal GH release. This contrasts with earlier GHRPs such as GHRP-2 and GHRP-6, which elevate cortisol and prolactin. Because it preserves the natural pulsatile pattern of GH secretion and relies on an intact pituitary, it is often paired with a GHRH analog for an additive or synergistic GH response.
benefits: Selective GH release with minimal cortisol, ACTH, or prolactin elevation
Stimulates a natural, pulsatile pattern of GH secretion rather than constant elevation
Studied for supporting lean body mass, recovery, and tissue repair via the GH/IGF-1 axis
Often reported to support sleep quality, consistent with nocturnal GH pulses
Synergizes with GHRH analogs (e.g. CJC-1295) for a larger combined GH pulse
Generally well tolerated relative to less selective GHRPs
risks: Not FDA-approved; no large phase 3 trials completed and no approved clinical indication
Banned by WADA and prohibited in competitive sport as a GH secretagogue
Stimulating the GH/IGF-1 axis is theoretically a concern with active or prior malignancy
Can transiently raise blood glucose and lower insulin sensitivity via GH effects
Human long-term safety data are limited; most evidence is preclinical or small short-term studies
sideEffects: Headache, flushing, or lightheadedness shortly after injection
Transient increase in appetite (ghrelin-receptor activity, milder than GHRP-6)
Water retention, tingling, or joint stiffness from elevated GH/IGF-1
Fatigue or drowsiness, especially with evening dosing

dosage.notes: Common educational protocol is 100-300 mcg per dose, often dosed at ~1 mcg/kg, given 1-3 times daily on an empty stomach (food, especially carbohydrate/fat, blunts the GH pulse). When stacked with a GHRH analog, each is typically drawn into the same syringe. This is educational information, not medical advice.

frequency: 1-3x daily, often before bed and/or post-workout, on an empty stomach
halfLife: Short; terminal half-life roughly 2 hours in human PK studies (reported ~2-3 hours at lower doses), with the GH pulse peaking ~30-60 minutes after dosing and returning to baseline within 2-3 hours
cycleLength: Commonly run in cycles of about 8-12 weeks with breaks, to limit receptor desensitization

reconstitution.notes: 5 mg vial + 2 mL bacteriostatic water = 2.5 mg/mL (2500 mcg/mL), so 250 mcg = 0.1 mL (10 units on a U-100 insulin syringe). Swirl gently; do not shake.

storage: Store lyophilized vials frozen or refrigerated. After reconstitution, refrigerate at 2-8C and use within ~4 weeks. Protect from light.
contraindications: Active or history of cancer (caution due to GH/IGF-1 axis stimulation)
Competitive athletes subject to anti-doping testing (WADA-prohibited)
Poorly controlled diabetes or insulin resistance (GH can raise glucose)

interactions.note: Classic GHRP + GHRH combination. Ipamorelin (GHRP) amplifies the GH pulse while CJC-1295 (GHRH analog) raises the baseline and broadens the release window; together they produce a larger, more sustained GH pulse than either alone, while ipamorelin keeps cortisol/prolactin low.

Tesamorelin is also a GHRH analog and shares the GH-axis mechanism with CJC-1295. Combining it with ipamorelin overlaps GH stimulation; stacking two GH-axis agents compounds effects on IGF-1, glucose, and fluid retention, so monitor accordingly rather than running multiple GHRH analogs at once.

references.title: Ipamorelin, the first selective growth hormone secretagogue (Raun et al., Eur J Endocrinol 1998, PubMed)
Pharmacokinetic-Pharmacodynamic Modeling of Ipamorelin, a Growth Hormone Releasing Peptide, in Human Volunteers (Pharm Res)
Pharmacokinetic evaluation of ipamorelin and other peptidyl growth hormone secretagogues with emphasis on nasal absorption (PubMed)
The GH secretagogues ipamorelin and GH-releasing peptide-6 enhance bone formation (PubMed)
